mascotLines: I only need one good sniff to know you're home — turns out logging in only needs one good code now, too.
I buried my favorite toy and it was still there when I dug it back up. Your saved payment methods work the same way now.
I stopped begging for treats I'm not allowed to have. Reminder emails stopped offering payment links you can't use, either.
Fetching things and dropping them exactly where they belong is basically my whole job — now it's the Upload Window's job too.

items.text: Automatic pricing updates for firms with special rates
items.desc: Before, moving a firm onto negotiated pricing (a lower processing rate, or a 0% ACH promo period) meant manual steps across Slack, spreadsheets, and Stripe. Now the process is automated, and firms get an automatic email confirming their new rate — plus another one when a promo period ends and pricing reverts to normal.

items.text: Drag and drop is back in the Desktop app's Upload Window
items.desc: You can once again drag files straight from Windows Explorer (or another app) into the Upload Window on the Desktop app — a feature that went missing when the app was rebuilt. Multiple files at once are supported too.

items.text: Fewer interruptions from the Document History popup
items.desc: If your firm mainly uses the Desktop app as a drive — dragging files into it rather than uploading through the browser — you'll see the Document History popup interrupt you far less often now.

items.text: Assign jobs by role, not just by name
items.desc: Pipeline automations can now assign jobs to whoever currently holds a role (like Preparer or Manager) instead of a fixed team member — so when staff change roles, you don't have to go update every automation by hand.

items.text: Some clients got logged out with an error during two-factor login
items.desc: If you had two browser tabs open at once during email-code login (or the mobile app quietly retried a request), you could hit an error instead of getting signed in. Fixed — this was affecting a meaningful number of logins.

items.text: Payment methods could get wiped out while creating a bill
items.desc: A settings sync bug could wipe a client's saved payment methods right as a bill was being created, causing the bill creation to fail. Fixed.

items.text: Editing a recurring invoice could throw a server error
items.desc: Updating certain recurring invoices could crash with a server error instead of showing a clear message. Fixed.

items.text: Invoice reminder emails offered to pay even when you can't accept payments
items.desc: Clients getting a reminder email saw a "Pay Invoice" link and payment instructions even for firms with no payment method enabled — leading to confused clients hitting a dead end. Now reminders only mention payment when it's actually possible.

items.text: CRM imports could tag the wrong account
items.desc: When two accounts had nearly identical names (like one with an extra space), importing tags could apply them to the wrong one — sometimes an archived account instead of the active one you meant. Fixed so the exact match wins.

items.text: Deleting an archived client behaved differently depending on how you deleted it
items.desc: Deleting an archived client one at a time could get blocked, while deleting that same client through bulk delete went through anyway. Both paths now follow the same rule.

items.text: Desktop app sync error for a small number of users
items.desc: A file-sync error was repeatedly hitting a handful of users during large folder syncs. Fixed.

items.text: Import was at risk of silently deleting country data
items.desc: Behind an in-progress import redesign, common country names like "United States" weren't recognized and would get wiped out during import instead of saved. Caught and fixed before it reached anyone's real data.

poll.label: Automatic pricing-update emails for special-rate firms
Drag-and-drop back in the Desktop Upload Window
Assigning jobs by role instead of by name

mystery: Bonus fix that didn't make the headline list: a CRM import counter was quietly under-reporting how many contacts didn't get linked to an account — off by exactly one skipped row. Tiny, but now it counts right.
